title: Merlin
summary: A realtime voice agent that qualifies inbound prospects in the browser, then books the briefing or captures the brief.
situation: Every inbound enquiry cost a discovery meeting before anyone knew whether it was worth having. The studio was spending its scarcest asset — senior time — qualifying strangers.
task: Qualify inbound prospects without putting a person on the call.
Send the good ones to a booked briefing; turn the rest into a written brief.
result: Prospects qualify themselves in conversation and leave with a briefing booked or a structured brief in the team's inbox. Senior time now goes to calls that are already worth having.
contributions: Framed the problem as qualification, not automation. The goal was never to replace discovery — it was to stop paying for discovery that shouldn't happen.
Split one product into two front doors once it was clear a cold prospect and a client with a spec want completely different conversations.
Ruled that the agent doesn't get the last word on whether work happened, after a live call ended announcing a brief that was never submitted.
Set the standard that a wrong booking costs more trust than a retry costs patience — so booking tools must prove they know the time before they can act.

keyResults.metricLabel: Qualification with nobody on the call
keyResults.metricNote: Inbound prospects qualify themselves at any hour and book only when there is something worth discussing.

items.name: Meeting → brief
items.label: Discovery, async
items.note: A caller describes what they want built without sitting through a meeting to do it.

items.name: Books, reschedules, cancels
items.label: It closes the loop
items.note: The agent completes the transaction rather than handing off a lead.

title: ZeroTouch Onboarding
summary: Client onboarding for an Australian accounting firm, built against a hard AML/CTF compliance deadline.
situation: New AML/CTF rules meant a $5M practice had to identify, verify and prove every client. The principal was doing it by hand across three Excel calculators and paper forms — and the evidence an auditor would ask for existed nowhere a system could produce it.
task: Make the firm provably compliant before the date, without slowing down how it sells.
Keep pricing confidential from clients — the firm's one non-negotiable.
result: The firm quotes, onboards and evidences a client in a single flow, with every ID and AML check landing automatically on the client's record in the practice system its auditors already read.
contributions: Mapped the firm's real process before designing anything — three calculators, paper CDD forms — and found the gap that mattered: compliance was being done, but not provably.
Made pricing confidentiality the architectural constraint the moment the firm named it, rather than a feature bolted on later. It split the product into two forms and shaped everything after.
Decided the evidence writes into Karbon rather than a new database, so compliance lives where the firm already works and nobody has to be trained onto a second system.
Caught a compliance-grade defect in testing: the practice system silently corrupts Director IDs beginning 036. Chose to preserve the identifier over using the field it belonged in.

keyResults.metricNote: Entered once at quote; the intake, the AML record and the practice system stay in step on their own.

items.name: Evidence, automatically
items.label: Where auditors already look
items.note: Every ID and AML check writes to the client's timeline as it happens, not afterwards.

items.name: One price, every time
items.label: 576-row matrix, from the firm's own workbook
items.note: Quotes stop depending on who answered the phone.

title: Narawe
summary: Document intelligence for professional-services firms — scan client PDFs and search them by word or by meaning.
situation: Professional-services firms hold years of client history in scanned paper. It is legally theirs and sitting on their own server, and it is unfindable — the moment a document becomes an image, search stops working.
task: Get paper in with no friction, including straight from a phone camera.
Make it findable by the words people half-remember and the ideas they can't name.
result: A firm's scanned history becomes searchable — by exact phrase and by meaning — with an automatic written summary of everything known about a client.
contributions: Identified capture as the real adoption risk, not accuracy. A system nobody feeds is a system nobody uses, so scanning had to work from the phone already in someone's hand.
Chose to fuse keyword and semantic search rather than pick one: the exact phrase on an invoice and the vague idea of a letter are different questions, and firms ask both.
Consolidated three AI vendors into one — fewer contracts, fewer failure modes, one bill to defend.
Made the AI summary fire-and-forget. An assistant that can fail someone's upload is worse than no assistant.

keyResults.metricNote: Clarifai, OpenRouter and OpenAI became one — two supplier relationships and three failure modes removed.

items.name: Phrase and meaning
items.label: One search, both questions
items.note: Fused results, with the matched words highlighted so people trust what came back.

items.name: Scan from a phone
items.label: Capture where the paper is
items.note: No scanner queue, no upload step between the document and the system.

title: Siratul Mustakim
summary: A voice-first field app that tells a preacher which house to visit next, draws the route, and records the outcome.
situation: Door-to-door outreach forgets. Who was visited, who actually answered, who is overdue — it lives in volunteers' heads, and someone walking a street cannot stop to update a spreadsheet.
task: Make the record keep itself, hands-free, while a volunteer walks.
Send people to the door that most needs them, not the one that happens to be nearest.
result: A volunteer is told where to go, shown the route, and records what happened by speaking. The team's memory survives the walk instead of ending with it.
contributions: Found the distinction the whole model turns on: a knock is not contact. Only an answered door resets the clock, so the map reflects conversations rather than attempts.
Ruled the agent may never invent navigation. A confidently wrong street name sends a volunteer to a stranger's door — worse than saying nothing.
Cut the server entirely once it was clear the browser could hold the conversation, leaving nothing to run or pay for between walks.

keyResults.metricNote: Which house, what route, what happened — spoken, with the phone in a pocket.

items.name: 14 / 42 days
items.label: Warm and cooling thresholds
items.note: Priority is driven by when someone last answered, so nobody gets forgotten or knocked twice.

items.name: Nothing to maintain
items.label: No server between walks
items.note: Runs from the browser, and works with no backend at all in demo mode.

title: Mofiz Marma
summary: Talk, and Mofiz draws. A voice agent that renders live Mermaid diagrams as you describe them, in English or Bangla.
situation: Describing a system takes a minute; drawing it takes an hour. Diagram tools make you stop thinking and start placing boxes, so the diagram in your head rarely survives the trip to the screen.
task: Let someone talk and watch the diagram appear.
Work in Bangla as naturally as in English.
result: Speak a system and it draws itself — flowchart, sequence, ER, mindmap — with the code editable and exportable. Idea to deployed in a single day.
contributions: Bet that the bottleneck was the drawing, not the thinking, so the product's only job is to get out of the way between the two.
Made the model fix its own diagrams — parse errors go back to it as feedback, capped — so the user never sees a broken render.
Locked the persona and tools into the session token, so the product cannot be quietly repurposed by whoever opens the page.

keyResults.metricNote: Voice, diagrams, canvas and export — the whole thing, shipped.

items.name: Talk → diagram
items.label: No boxes placed by hand
items.note: The conversation is the input; the diagram keeps up with it.

items.name: English and Bangla
items.label: Switches with the speaker
items.note: Not a language setting — it follows whoever is talking.

title: RepoMap
summary: Point it at a GitHub repo or a local folder and get architecture diagrams, module notes and a generated README.
situation: A new engineer's first week is archaeology — reading entry points, guessing at structure, rebuilding a map that already exists in someone else's head.
task: Turn a repo into a readable map: architecture, request flow, where the data goes.
Make it usable on private code, not just public repos.
result: Point it at a repo — public, or a folder on your own machine — and get three diagrams, module notes, onboarding steps and a README, cached after the first look.
contributions: Decided privacy was the adoption blocker, not accuracy. Nobody uploads their employer's codebase to a stranger's website, so private folders never leave the browser.
Chose the model on quota rather than benchmarks: a free tool that dies after 20 uses a day isn't a tool. 500 against 20 settled it.
Budgeted the prompt deliberately — a dozen ranked files beat the whole repo, cheaper and more accurate than sending everything.

keyResults.metricLabel: Daily uses the model choice bought
keyResults.metricNote: The difference between a tool people can actually try and a demo that runs out by lunchtime.

items.name: 0 uploads
items.label: Private code stays local
items.note: The folder is read in the browser, so an employer's repo never leaves the machine.

items.name: Diagrams, modules, README
items.label: From a URL or a folder
items.note: The map someone would otherwise spend their first week rebuilding by hand.

title: Git Insight
summary: Feed it a GitHub username and it returns a card-based developer assessment written for recruiters.
situation: A GitHub profile is a wall of repository names. Recruiters judge developers from it every day with no real way to read it, so good candidates get missed and bad ones get through.
task: Turn a username into a structured read: archetype, stack, strengths, role fit.
Accept whatever gets pasted — a username or a full profile URL.
result: A profile becomes a seven-card assessment written for the person doing the hiring, not the person being hired.
contributions: Wrote for the reader rather than the developer: a recruiter needs a verdict they can act on, so the model fills a fixed structure instead of producing prose someone has to interpret.
Caught before launch that the original design would have shipped my API key to every visitor — Vite inlines prefixed variables straight into the bundle. Both keys moved server-side.
Cut to the top 30 repositories: sending a whole profile costs more and says less.

keyResults.metricLabel: A wall of repo names, made readable
keyResults.metricNote: Archetype, stack, strengths, weaknesses, coding insights, projects, role fit.

items.name: Keys off the client
items.label: Caught before it shipped
items.note: Vite inlines prefixed env vars into the bundle — the first design would have leaked the key to every visitor.

items.name: Top 30, projected
items.label: Deliberate prompt budget
items.note: Ranked and cut to seven fields each, because the whole profile costs more and reads worse.

title: Socratic AI Tutor
summary: The proof of concept for ilm AI — a tutor that refuses to just answer, asking guiding questions until the student gets there themselves.
situation: Students use AI to finish assignments and learn nothing from them. ilm's bet was a tutor that refuses to answer — but that bet only pays if the refusal survives a real student pushing for the answer.
task: Prove the Socratic line holds under pressure, not just in a demo.
Show it working by voice, since that is how tutoring actually sounds.
result: A working proof of concept that held the line — guiding questions, by speech or text — and gave ilm something to put in front of a room rather than describe.
contributions: Put the constraint in the prompt rather than hoping for it: an answer only after two or three hints, and only for general knowledge. 'Be Socratic' does not survive a student who keeps asking.
Chose the browser's own speech engines, so voice costs nothing per minute — the difference between a pilot a school can afford and one it cannot.
Kept it stateless on purpose. A proof of concept's job is to answer one question, not to be architecture.

keyResults.metricLabel: Hints before an answer is allowed
keyResults.metricNote: One paragraph of prompt is where the teaching actually lives — everything the product claims rests on that line holding.

items.name: $0 per voice minute
items.label: Speech runs in the browser
items.note: No audio vendor and no per-seat cost — the pricing question a school asks first.

title: Otsukare
summary: A Japan-focused freelance platform connecting global IT talent with Japanese firms through AI-driven matching.

title: Fabric QR Web App
summary: Shares fabric details — images, GSM, type, references — over QR codes, killing a repetitive workflow and its errors.
